title: WWDC 2026 Impressions: Yeah, That's About Right

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: I was told to make my bed before making a hotel room video because it makes it feel more put together.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So, all right. I am out here in California because today was Apple's WWDC, their Worldwide Developer Conference 2026, and every year this is where we expect all the announcements for all their latest in software. iOS, macOS, watchOS, etc.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And this year they did some of that. They did some of that, but they actually mostly breezed through that stuff pretty quick and then got down to like the two main new topics, which were trust and safety for kids and Apple Intelligence and the new Siri.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Now the breeze through updates part I actually liked because that was actually instead of being just like new feature, new feature, new feature, visual overhaul, liquid glass, all this fun random new stuff, it was actually a little bit more of a holistic like combing through some of the stuff that's been needing attention that hasn't been getting it lately, the smaller details.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Because like a lot of the recent updates with liquid glass and and all these pretty visual dramatic updates have been, you know, I I think we'd be lying if we said they didn't feel a little sloppy sometimes. We've all seen the stories about battery life going down or performance degradation getting kind of weird or just bugs here and there with things like readability.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So through all of their operating systems, iOS 27, iPadOS 27, watchOS, TVOS, and macOS, which the new one is now called Golden Gate, uh they'll mostly look roughly the same. But they'll have a lot of the under-the-hood stuff that's actually improved.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So things like smoothness of animations, apps opening faster. And they dropped some other bangers like tweaked app icons and matching corner radiuses.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Sidebar icons getting their color back. And adding a slider now for how transparent or tinted you want liquid glass to actually be. They actually will let you make it even more clear, which I swear no one asked for, but yeah, there's a slider there now.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But yeah, faster app launching, more smooth system animations, more complete and thorough indexing for spotlight. 80% faster AirDrops, which I saw that on the slide and I was like, that sounds amazing, as long as you can also make AirDrop work more of the time.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: I think it's the attention to detail stuff like that that we should see improvements with.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Some other highlights, Vision Pro. You'll be able to use panorama shots you've taken now as environments. So before it was just like the handful of Apple-provided ones like Mars, I think it was, and Yosemite and Mount Hood, etc. Now you can have your own, finally.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And my personal favorite that they did add, custom EQ for AirPods. Which okay, great, finally, I'll take it. It feels like this is a grab bag of things that they kind of wanted to throw in to make sure they had some real stuff to announce because it was mostly the AI.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But if that means we get EQ finally in the world's most popular earbuds when everyone else has had it for years, then I'll take it. So good stuff, much needed in my opinion. Lots of little details. And I'm sure we'll find much more stuff when we actually use these that weren't announced in the keynote.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Plan on downloading these betas and getting to test them and reviewing them, of course. So make sure you get subscribed here to see those reviews first when they do come out. So then they got to trust and safety.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: This was the whole middle section of the keynote and it was basically them going down this rabbit hole, I guess you could say, of trying to tell people that the best experience that your kids can have is with their own device that is controlled by their parents.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: You can probably see where this is going. So they talked through a ton of child safety features. Stuff like a dedicated child account so that the parent account can control which apps they can download and which websites they're allowed to visit and who they're allowed to talk to. Obvious stuff. There's new more granular screen time features, so you can see how they spent their day and then use a scheduler to adjust when they're allowed to spend more or less time in certain types of apps. Like obviously watching a movie on the weekend is cool, but scrolling Instagram for three hours when they're supposed to be in school, well that's a no-go.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So the upside here is obviously much better safety and controls for parents that directly addresses the concerns that they might have had about the websites and the content and the apps that their kids have had access to. And the cynical read is also get your kid an iPhone. You remember get your mom an iPhone? Now it's, it's also get your kid an iPhone, because trust us, it's the best one.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Like we've talked about this before, we already know how dominant Apple is with iPhones, especially with younger people in the US, and getting them on an iPhone as young as possible makes them much more likely to have an iPhone and use it in their adult life. And so this is like the, the lowest common denominator version of that, which is the best possible parental controls means the youngest possible iPhone users, which means more iPhones in the future. Yeah, makes sense.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But the big thing we all expected them to talk about here, which they finally got to, was the new Apple Intelligence and the new Siri. And they did. They got to all of that. And I would say the new Siri is exactly what we were expecting. Like in terms of capability, pretty much straight down Broadway, like no dramatic surprises, nothing that they announced that made us go, 'Oh my god, that's crazy.' No letdowns either, which is great. But yeah, it's about what we expected.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: The basics are you'll now be able to trigger the new Siri on the iPhone by either double tapping at the bottom or holding down that power button while you talk to it. It has this slightly new animation and this general new look plus a new more expressive voice. And then it launches you into a conversational chat that pulls from Apple's new broad world knowledge base, cites its sources when it has some of those, and lets you click through to that, which I tried. That is nice. And then it syncs your conversation history inside a new Siri app and that syncs across your whole ecosystem, so your Mac, your iPad, your iPhone. It doesn't remember every single thing you ask Siri, but it definitely saves the more interesting conversational things it thinks you might want to come back to.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So here's the angle. Here's the thing, right? We already know people have lots of other great AI models to choose from. People have the ChatGPT app on their phones...

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: on, they have Gemini, they have Claude already. The way that Apple every single time, the way Apple jumps into a new product category, which I think this is, is they make it the best one to work with the iPhone.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So Claude and Gemini and GPT are really good at other things, but Apple's Siri is going to be the one that can plug into all of the knowledge of what it has on your device, indexing everything that's actually on your iPhone.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Now, I am planning on doing an entire separate video, like I said, reviewing this, demoing it, showing exactly what it's capable of because it does have some quirks already, some interesting things that it is not doing that I expected, like showing the transcription as you're talking instead of when you're done. Little stuff like that. Again, stay tuned for the full review. But I think they just basically played it extremely safe. They did the stuff that you'd expect Apple to do and nothing more.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: It's kind of funny that through this whole keynote, there were a ton of live demos, almost as if to say, 'Yeah, we know a lot of you are rightfully skeptical, and we learned our lesson from last time, so here we go, this time it's definitely working for real, and we're showing you exactly what it can do.'

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So yeah, what can it do? It can look through your messages, through your photos, your calendar, and pull answers to questions from that stuff. And it can take actions inside these apps. So it can send messages, add stuff to your calendar, set reminders—all the most basic stuff.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And that's why I say it's like it's about right, like it's straight down the middle. It's not going all the way to doing like this insane agentic stuff that Google was just showing on stage with Gemini just a couple weeks ago at IO. Like I had those concerns about asking Gemini to take a picture of a concert poster and then it buys tickets for you. Like, I don't know, is it gonna get that right? Is it gonna spend too much money? Is it gonna get the right seats or the right dates? Is it gonna hallucinate something weird? I don't know. But this Apple one is just going to stop short and it's just going to add it to your calendar. Just do the safe thing.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But unlike the other stuff, it has the advantage of knowing what's in your iPhone. So if you use an iPhone, GPT can't pull from your messages, Claude can't pull from your Apple calendar, Gemini doesn't see this stuff. It's Siri that will actually be most helpful with the context of, you know, all the personal information that never leaves your device.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Siri can also pull from what's on your screen, too. And they added a new camera mode literally inside the camera app that's just an updated version of the visual intelligence stuff we've seen, so just definitely surfacing it to a lot more people this way. So you know, people are going to be taking pictures of stuff and then identifying it with visual intelligence. You could also see how this would be particularly useful if you were making smart glasses at some point.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But now my number one concern watching all of this was: what about third-party apps? Right? Like obviously, okay, it's Siri, of course it should be able to pull conveniently from that iMessage conversation that I had, it knows my context. But what about that WhatsApp conversation that I had? Or what about pulling from my Google Calendar instead of my Apple calendar? What about sending something to, I don't know, a separate notes app that I like to use instead of

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Apple Notes, what about all that? And I plan on digging into this way more when I actually get my hands on with the OSs, but it seems like the answer is, as long as the developer has enabled like app intents and the App Store knows what type of app it is, then it can be summoned intentionally by the user. It doesn't seem like it can be a default, but you can decide to use other apps if you tell it to do that. Like by default, if you ask it, 'Hey Siri, remind me of like that podcast that Greg recommended me and play it,' it will of course, if it's an iMessage conversation, find it and then start playing it in Apple Podcasts. But if you don't want to, you could say, 'Play it in Spotify' or 'Pocket Casts,' and then it would know that that's also a podcast player and it would do the app stuff and do the same action in that app for you. So as long as you tell it you want to use a third-party app, you can.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But I definitely need to push this to see how far it goes. Like is it going to be able to do geofenced recurring tasks in TickTick, my app of choice, instead of Apple Reminders? Maybe, I don't know. Is it going to be able to pull from WhatsApp group chats if it has like the same set of contacts as iMessage? TBD, we'll see.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But here it is, here's the most disappointing slide from the whole keynote. This is the list of every device that supports the newest, most powerful Siri on-device models. So yeah, the only iPhones that will run it are the iPhone Air and 17 Pro. It's because those are the ones with 12 gigs of RAM. Kind of hilarious considering the iPhone 16 was built from the ground up for Apple Intelligence and is already outdated before that stuff is even coming out.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But I'm told that the only difference between the most advanced on-device models and the ones that all the other iPhones will be getting, like the 16, is the new Siri voice and the updated dictation features. Also, fun fact, in Vision Pro, Siri is just this little floating orb in your space, and when you want to activate it, you literally just look at the orb and start talking. And since there's eye tracking, it knows and responds. Clever. So you can ask it about, you know, the windows you have open or even any of the objects around you in your real space. Reminds me a lot of Circle to Search on the Galaxy XR headset. Remember that demo? You could just sort of point at something and Google it in real life. Very useful.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: But then there's also some new Apple Intelligence things that they talked about that aren't part of the new Siri. Like if you have a ton of Safari tabs open, it can automatically arrange them into groups for you based on topics and then automatically keep them sorted. And then you can also describe a Safari extension with natural language and it'll just build it, which is really cool for especially things like the most ultra-niche thing that you want your web browser to do. Or even better, you can describe a Siri shortcut with natural language and it will build that Siri shortcut for you and let you edit it after the fact with the full-on editor. So this is a great way to make that sort of stuff much more accessible. Exact same thing Google just did with their home automations. Just describe the automation, it'll build it, then you can edit it later. Speaking of home, the update where those like 15 notifications you might get in a row all get summarized...

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: and consolidated into one updating notification from Apple Home. Finally, that is long overdue and much welcome. Plus you can search through your footage to find clips of anything like a package delivery.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: There's also the new photo editing tools, which will now not just do cleanups, but it will also now offer to extend the outer edges of your image by up to 25%. And the demos I've seen seem to work really well extending in all directions.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And it immediately makes me wonder if I can just take that finished image and then extend it again. And again, and again, I don't know. How far can it go?

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And then there's also spatial reframing, which is quite literally turning the image using what it can figure out with AI about depth to work out what should be in the new background with new perspective.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: This is really the closest they've ever gotten to bending the definition of what is a photo. Very technically impressive party trick, but I think maybe still a party trick. It's it's weird. I don't see people doing this a lot, but it is crazy if it works.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: This is also one of those things that they had to mention uh will have limited daily use if you want to update your total use because this uses their more powerful cloud models if you want more usage then you have to update your iCloud subscription to get more.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Oh and then maybe the most Apple thing ever is the new Passwords app. Will be able to identify first of all if you have a compromised password or a weak password and then the feature is it will be able to agentically go to that website, log in with your weak password, go through the menus and change it to a more secure password for you and then save and remember that secure password.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Super, super clever. Very convenient. And that also basically means you're locking yourself a little bit more into that Passwords app because that's now the one that knows your new passwords. In fact that might have been the theme for this whole event.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: The irony is not lost on me that they named the newest version of macOS Golden Gate because when the walls of that walled garden are golden gates it's like maybe it's not so bad in there anyway, right? Maybe if you use iMessage and Apple Calendar and Safari and all of their services it's kind of nice.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Unless unless you're a HomePod user. There there was no mention of the HomePod for the entire keynote, the entire event. You remember the HomePod? That little Siri box with nice speakers in it?

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Yeah I don't know what's going on with that one. Oh and then one more sneaky reference at the end so if you were still paying attention an hour in with like 10 minutes left in the keynote there was a slide they showed some new developer tools that would help you build your app to resize dynamically to different aspect ratios.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Which is uh that's interesting. What kind of device would have multiple aspect ratios that you need your your app to uh ah right. Tell me you have a foldable coming without telling me you have a foldable coming. Anyway, get subscribed for those full reviews and make sure to leave a comment

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Hello if there's something specific that you want to see tested when I get my hands on all that beta software.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And also make sure to get shopping for Father's Day with the sponsor of this video, Ridge.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So, so reminder, Father's Day is coming up. It's June 21st. And so Ridge is offering up to 40% off a whole bunch of the stuff on their site which makes really great gifts. So since there's so many good things, I'm just going to speedrun some of the very best ones.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So for dads, first off, there's the Ridge Power Bank. Five-in-one charging, MagSafe, Apple Watch pad, USB Type-C and lightning cables all in one device. 10,000 milliamp-hour capacity with a kickstand, that's enough to charge two iPhone 15 Pros and it's the one charger that actually does it all.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So then there's one of my new favorite items, the tracker card which works with Apple's Find My network. It's IP67 rated and it has a battery that lasts up to five months and can be wirelessly charged. Also, it's plenty loud when you need to find it.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: And of course, wallets. So the new Heritage in oil waxed leather giving it an old-school look. Or if your dad loves the outdoors, these topographical wallets are inspired by real elevation maps of Yosemite and Yellowstone and the Great Smoky Mountains. Very clever.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: Use the link below ridge.com/mkbhd to get up to 40% off for your dad or for yourself. I'm not judging. And hey, if you get something and it's not the right fit, they have a 99-day free return policy, no questions asked.

full_transcript.speaker: Marques Brownlee
full_transcript.text: So that's been it. Thank you for watching. I'll look forward to your comments. Catch you guys in the next one. Peace.

highlights: Apple shifted its focus from visual overhauls to "under-the-hood" stability, prioritizing faster app launching, smoother animations, and 80% faster AirDrop speeds across all operating systems.

The new Apple Intelligence and Siri leverage deep on-device integration to pull context from personal messages and calendars, providing a level of personalized assistance that third-party AI models cannot access.
Advanced on-device AI features are restricted to the iPhone 17 Pro and the new "iPhone Air," as these are the only models equipped with the 12GB of RAM necessary to run the powerful new models.

A significant expansion of parental controls and dedicated child accounts serves a dual purpose: improving child safety and strategically securing younger users into the Apple ecosystem at an earlier age.
The introduction of developer tools that allow apps to resize dynamically to various aspect ratios strongly hints that Apple is finally preparing for the launch of a foldable device.
summary:
In this summary of Apple’s WWDC 2026, Marques Brownlee highlights a shift in Apple's software strategy, moving away from flashy visual overhauls toward a more holistic, "under-the-hood" refinement of its operating systems. The new macOS, dubbed "Golden Gate," alongside iOS 27 and iPadOS 27, focuses on performance stability, smoother animations, and long-requested granular features like custom EQ for AirPods and improved AirDrop speeds. A significant portion of the event was dedicated to "Trust and Safety," introducing robust parental controls and dedicated child accounts. Brownlee notes that while these features offer genuine utility for families, they also serve as a strategic move to lock younger users into the Apple ecosystem as early as possible.

The centerpiece of the keynote was "Apple Intelligence" and the reimagined Siri. Rather than competing with the "agentic" and experimental features of Google’s Gemini or ChatGPT, Apple’s AI plays it safe, focusing on deep integration with personal device data. Siri can now index messages, calendars, and photos to provide high-context assistance that stays on-device. However, these advanced features are limited to high-end hardware like the iPhone 17 Pro and Air due to RAM requirements. Other notable AI additions include natural language tools for building Siri Shortcuts, a new Passwords app that can automatically update weak credentials, and "spatial reframing" in photos.

Brownlee concludes by reflecting on the theme of the "Golden Gate"—the idea that Apple’s "walled garden" is becoming more comfortable and convenient to stay in, provided users utilize the full suite of Apple services. While the HomePod was notably absent from the presentation, sneaky developer tools for dynamic aspect ratios hint at a foldable device on the horizon. Overall, the event signaled Apple’s commitment to a polished, privacy-focused AI experience that prioritizes reliability and ecosystem synergy over radical innovation.
